entries.name: Mimikatz
entries.summary: Post-exploitation tool that dumps LSASS for plaintext passwords, NTLM hashes, and Kerberos tickets. Supports DCSync, Golden/Silver Ticket, and Pass-the-Hash.

entries.name: Cobalt Strike
entries.summary: Commercial adversary simulation framework using Beacon implants for C2. Supports lateral movement, credential harvesting, and payload staging via HTTP/S, DNS, and SMB channels.

entries.name: Impacket
entries.summary: Python toolkit for crafting network protocols. Includes PsExec, WMIExec, SMBExec, SecretsDump, and NTLMRelayx for remote execution, credential dumping, and relay attacks.

entries.name: BloodHound
entries.summary: AD reconnaissance tool that maps attack paths via graph analysis. SharpHound collector enumerates users, groups, sessions, ACLs, and trust relationships.

entries.name: Rubeus
entries.summary: C# Kerberos abuse toolkit. Performs Kerberoasting, AS-REP Roasting, constrained/unconstrained delegation abuse, and ticket manipulation.

entries.name: CrackMapExec / NetExec
entries.summary: Network pentest tool for mass authentication testing across SMB, WinRM, LDAP, MSSQL. Used for password spraying, credential validation, and command execution.

entries.name: PsExec
entries.summary: Sysinternals remote execution utility. Creates a temporary service on the target via admin shares (ADMIN$). Widely used legitimately and by attackers for lateral movement.

entries.name: PowerSploit / PowerView
entries.summary: PowerShell post-exploitation framework. PowerView module provides AD enumeration (users, groups, GPOs, trusts). Often loaded reflectively to avoid disk detection.

entries.name: SharpHound
entries.summary: BloodHound's .NET data collector. Queries LDAP, enumerates sessions, local groups, and ACLs across the domain to map attack paths.

entries.name: Certify / Certipy
entries.summary: AD Certificate Services exploitation tools. Enumerate vulnerable templates and exploit ESC1-ESC8 misconfigurations for privilege escalation via certificate abuse.

entries.name: Sliver
entries.summary: Open-source C2 framework with mTLS, WireGuard, HTTP/S, and DNS transports. Supports process injection, pivoting, and extension loading.

entries.name: Brute Ratel
entries.summary: Commercial C2 framework designed for EDR evasion. Badger implants use syscall-level techniques to avoid userland API hooks.

entries.name: LaZagne
entries.summary: Multi-platform credential recovery tool. Extracts passwords from browsers, WiFi, mail clients, databases, and Windows credential stores.

entries.name: Seatbelt
entries.summary: GhostPack .NET host-survey tool. Collects OS info, installed software, firewall rules, browser data, credential files, and security product status.

entries.name: Responder
entries.summary: LLMNR/NBT-NS/mDNS poisoner and rogue authentication server. Captures NetNTLM hashes by responding to broadcast name resolution queries.

entries.name: Covenant
entries.summary: .NET C2 framework using Grunt implants. Supports task-based execution, SMB lateral movement, and .NET assembly loading.

entries.name: ADFind
entries.summary: Command-line AD query tool. Used by attackers for domain enumeration — users, computers, OUs, trusts, subnets, and GPOs via LDAP queries.

entries.name: Chisel
entries.summary: TCP/UDP tunnel over HTTP using SSH protocol. Used for pivoting and port forwarding through firewalls to reach internal networks.

entries.name: LSASS Dump
entries.summary: Dumping the LSASS process memory to extract credentials. Methods include procdump, comsvcs.dll MiniDump, Task Manager, and direct memory reads.

entries.name: Kerberoasting
entries.summary: Requesting TGS tickets for SPNs and cracking them offline to recover service account passwords. No special privileges required beyond domain authentication.

entries.name: AS-REP Roasting
entries.summary: Requesting AS-REP for accounts with Kerberos pre-authentication disabled, then cracking the encrypted portion offline for the password.

entries.name: DCSync
entries.summary: Simulating domain controller replication via DRSUAPI to pull password hashes from AD. Requires Replicating Directory Changes privileges.

entries.name: Golden Ticket
entries.summary: Forging a TGT using the compromised KRBTGT hash. Grants unrestricted domain access and persists until the KRBTGT password is reset twice.

entries.name: Silver Ticket
entries.summary: Forging a TGS for a specific service using its NTLM hash. Bypasses KDC and provides targeted access without touching the domain controller.

entries.name: Pass-the-Hash
entries.summary: Authenticating with an NTLM hash instead of a plaintext password. Enables lateral movement without knowing the actual password.

entries.name: Pass-the-Ticket
entries.summary: Injecting stolen Kerberos tickets into a session to impersonate another user. Works with both TGTs and service tickets.

entries.name: DLL Sideloading
entries.summary: Placing a malicious DLL where a legitimate program will load it due to search order. Executes attacker code under a trusted process.

entries.name: Process Injection
entries.summary: Injecting code into a running process for execution under its context. Techniques include CreateRemoteThread, process hollowing, and APC injection.

entries.name: NTLM Relay
entries.summary: Forwarding captured NTLM authentication to another service. Combined with coercion attacks (PetitPotam, PrinterBug) to escalate privileges.

entries.name: Scheduled Task Persistence
entries.summary: Creating or modifying scheduled tasks to execute payloads at system startup, user logon, or on a recurring schedule for persistence.

entries.name: WMI Persistence
entries.summary: Using WMI event subscriptions (filter + consumer + binding) to trigger payload execution on system events. Survives reboots without file-based indicators.

entries.name: Living Off the Land (LOLBins)
entries.summary: Using built-in Windows binaries for malicious purposes — downloading payloads, executing code, or bypassing application controls without dropping custom tools.

entries.name: PowerShell Cradle
entries.summary: One-liner PowerShell commands that download and execute code in memory. Often encoded or obfuscated to evade detection. IEX(New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString pattern.

entries.name: Log Clearing / Indicator Removal
entries.summary: Clearing Windows event logs to remove evidence of activity. Done via wevtutil, Clear-EventLog, or direct manipulation of .evtx files.

entries.name: Registry Run Key Persistence
entries.summary: Adding entries to HKLM/HKCU Run or RunOnce registry keys to execute programs at user logon. One of the most common persistence mechanisms.

entries.name: Service Creation Persistence
entries.summary: Installing a new Windows service that runs a malicious binary. Executes as SYSTEM and persists across reboots. Commonly seen with PsExec.

entries.name: Token Impersonation
entries.summary: Stealing or manipulating Windows access tokens to escalate privileges or impersonate other users. Potato family exploits abuse service account tokens for SYSTEM.

entries.name: DPAPI Abuse
entries.summary: Abusing Windows Data Protection API to decrypt user secrets (browser passwords, saved credentials). Domain backup key allows decrypting any domain user's DPAPI blobs.

entries.name: Shadow Credentials
entries.summary: Adding rogue key credentials to a target's msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute, then using PKINIT to authenticate as that target and obtain their NTLM hash.

entries.name: AD CS Escalation (ESC1-ESC8)
entries.summary: Exploiting misconfigured AD Certificate Services templates to obtain certificates for arbitrary users, enabling domain privilege escalation.

entries.name: LSASS Memory
entries.summary: Local Security Authority Subsystem Service — the Windows process that handles authentication and stores credentials in memory. Primary target for credential dumping.

entries.name: NTDS.dit
entries.summary: The Active Directory database file on domain controllers containing all domain objects including password hashes. Extractable via ntdsutil, Volume Shadow Copy, or DCSync.

entries.name: SAM Database
entries.summary: Security Accounts Manager — registry hive storing local user account password hashes. Extractable via reg save, Volume Shadow Copy, or in-memory techniques.

entries.name: Prefetch
entries.summary: Windows artifact (.pf files in C:\Windows\Prefetch) that records program execution with timestamps, run counts, and loaded files. Key evidence for timeline analysis.

entries.name: Master File Table (MFT)
entries.summary: NTFS metadata structure recording all file entries with creation, modification, access, and entry-modified timestamps. Critical for filesystem timeline forensics.

entries.name: USN Journal
entries.summary: NTFS Update Sequence Number Journal — logs filesystem changes (create, delete, rename, write) with timestamps. Survives file deletion and is key for tracking file activity.

entries.name: Shimcache / AppCompatCache
entries.summary: Registry-based artifact tracking executables the OS checked for compatibility. Records file path, size, and last-modified timestamp. Proves file existed on disk.

entries.name: Amcache
entries.summary: Registry hive recording application execution and installation with SHA1 hashes, file paths, and timestamps. Persists after file deletion.

entries.name: SRUM (System Resource Usage Monitor)
entries.summary: Windows database tracking 30-60 days of application resource usage — network bytes sent/received per-app, CPU time, and energy usage. Proves application activity.

entries.name: Volume Shadow Copy
entries.summary: Windows snapshot service preserving point-in-time copies of files. Attackers use it to extract SAM/NTDS.dit; defenders use it to recover deleted evidence.

entries.name: Windows Event Forwarding (WEF)
entries.summary: Built-in Windows mechanism to forward event logs from source computers to a collector. Enables centralized log analysis without third-party agents.

entries.name: ETW (Event Tracing for Windows)
entries.summary: Windows kernel-level tracing framework. Security products hook ETW providers for visibility. Attackers may patch ETW to blind detection (ETW tampering).

entries.name: AMSI (Antimalware Scan Interface)
entries.summary: Windows API that exposes script content to security products before execution. Covers PowerShell, VBScript, JScript, and .NET. Targeted for bypass by attackers.

entries.name: Lateral Movement
entries.summary: Post-compromise technique of moving from one system to another within a network. Common methods: RDP, PsExec, WMI, SMB, WinRM, DCOM, scheduled tasks.

entries.name: Privilege Escalation
entries.summary: Gaining higher-level permissions than initially obtained. Local privesc targets SYSTEM; domain privesc targets Domain Admin. Methods include token abuse, exploits, and misconfigs.

entries.name: Persistence
entries.summary: Techniques ensuring continued access after initial compromise. Survives reboots, password changes, or remediation. Common: services, tasks, registry, DLL hijack, WMI.

entries.name: Indicators of Compromise (IOCs)
entries.summary: Observable artifacts indicating a security breach — file hashes, IP addresses, domain names, registry keys, or behavioral patterns used for detection and hunting.

entries.name: Threat Hunting
entries.summary: Proactive security practice of searching for threats not caught by automated detections. Uses hypotheses, behavioral analytics, and anomaly detection across log data.

entries.name: MITRE ATT&CK
entries.summary: Knowledge base of adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) based on real-world observations. 14 tactics from Reconnaissance through Impact.

entries.name: Sigma Rules
entries.summary: Generic, open-source signature format for SIEM detection rules. Platform-agnostic YAML format that can be converted to Splunk SPL, Elastic KQL, and others.

entries.name: YARA Rules
entries.summary: Pattern-matching tool for malware classification. Rules define byte patterns, strings, and conditions to identify malicious files or memory content.

entries.name: Timeline Analysis
entries.summary: Forensic technique of assembling timestamped artifacts (events, files, registry) into chronological order to reconstruct attacker activity and dwell time.

entries.name: Memory Forensics
entries.summary: Analyzing RAM captures to find running processes, network connections, injected code, encryption keys, and artifacts invisible on disk. Key tool: Volatility.

entries.name: Unconstrained Delegation
entries.summary: AD misconfiguration where a server caches client TGTs. Attackers coerce high-privilege accounts (e.g. DC) to authenticate, then steal their TGT for impersonation.

entries.name: Constrained Delegation Abuse
entries.summary: Abusing S4U2Self/S4U2Proxy Kerberos extensions to impersonate users to specific services. Resource-Based Constrained Delegation (RBCD) is exploitable with write access to msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity.

entries.name: Group Policy Abuse
entries.summary: Exploiting Group Policy for persistence or lateral movement. Includes GPP password recovery (cpassword), malicious GPO creation for code execution across OUs.

entries.name: BITS Job Abuse
entries.summary: Abusing Background Intelligent Transfer Service for stealthy file downloads and persistent execution. BITS jobs survive reboots and transfer data at low priority.

entries.name: Print Spooler Exploitation
entries.summary: Exploiting the Windows Print Spooler service for remote code execution (PrintNightmare) or NTLM coercion (PrinterBug/SpoolSample) for relay attacks.

entries.name: COM/DCOM Abuse
entries.summary: Using COM/DCOM objects for lateral movement and code execution. Abusable objects include MMC20.Application, ShellWindows, and ShellBrowserWindow.

entries.name: Metasploit / Meterpreter
entries.summary: Open-source exploitation framework with the Meterpreter in-memory payload. Provides exploit modules, post-exploitation tooling, process migration, and pivoting.

entries.name: Empire / Starkiller
entries.summary: PowerShell and Python C2 framework with a web UI (Starkiller). Provides modules for credential theft, lateral movement, and AD enumeration via in-memory PowerShell agents.

entries.name: Mythic
entries.summary: Multi-agent C2 framework with a web UI and pluggable payloads (Apollo for .NET, Athena for cross-platform, Poseidon for macOS/Linux). Designed for collaborative red-team operations.

entries.name: Havoc
entries.summary: Modern open-source C2 framework using the Demon implant. Supports indirect syscalls, sleep obfuscation, and modular extensions; observed in real-world intrusions.

entries.name: Volatility
entries.summary: Open-source memory forensics framework. Parses RAM captures to enumerate processes, network connections, loaded modules, injected code, and credential material.

entries.name: KAPE
entries.summary: Kroll Artifact Parser and Extractor. Collects forensic artifacts (registry, event logs, browser data, prefetch) from live or mounted systems and runs parsing modules.

entries.name: Velociraptor
entries.summary: Open-source endpoint visibility and DFIR platform using VQL (Velociraptor Query Language) to hunt across fleets. Collects artifacts, monitors processes, and runs YARA scans at scale.

entries.name: Hashcat
entries.summary: GPU-accelerated password cracker supporting hundreds of hash modes (NTLM, NetNTLMv2, Kerberos TGS, AS-REP). Standard tool for cracking output of Kerberoasting and AS-REP Roasting.

entries.name: AMSI Bypass
entries.summary: Disabling the Antimalware Scan Interface to evade in-memory script scanning. Common methods include patching amsi.dll!AmsiScanBuffer in memory or setting amsiInitFailed via reflection.

entries.name: ETW Patching
entries.summary: Patching ntdll!EtwEventWrite (or related providers) in memory to suppress Event Tracing for Windows telemetry. Blinds EDR products that rely on ETW for visibility.

entries.name: UAC Bypass
entries.summary: Abusing auto-elevating Windows binaries or COM objects to obtain a high-integrity process without a UAC prompt. Common targets include fodhelper.exe, eventvwr.exe, sdclt.exe, and ICMLuaUtil.

entries.name: BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver)
entries.summary: Loading a legitimately signed but vulnerable kernel driver to gain ring-0 execution. Used to disable EDR/AV from the kernel; abused drivers include RTCore64, gdrv, and various OEM utilities.

entries.name: Coercion Attacks
entries.summary: Forcing a Windows host (often a domain controller) to authenticate to an attacker-controlled endpoint via MS-EFSR, MS-RPRN, MS-DFSNM, or MS-FSRVP. Output is typically relayed (NTLM Relay) for privilege escalation.

entries.name: Password Spraying
entries.summary: Attempting a small number of common passwords against many accounts to avoid account lockout. Targets SMB, LDAP, OWA, ADFS, AzureAD, and on-prem Kerberos endpoints.

entries.name: Web Shell Deployment
entries.summary: Dropping a small server-side script (ASPX, PHP, JSP) into a web-accessible directory to provide persistent remote command execution. Often runs under the web server process (w3wp.exe on IIS).

entries.name: Ransomware Lifecycle
entries.summary: End-to-end intrusion pattern: initial access, recon, credential theft, lateral movement, data exfiltration, shadow copy and backup destruction, then mass file encryption with a ransom note.

entries.name: Shadow Copy Deletion
entries.summary: Deleting Volume Shadow Copies via vssadmin, wmic, or PowerShell to prevent file recovery. A near-universal precursor to ransomware encryption and a strong anti-forensics signal.

entries.name: Defender Tampering
entries.summary: Disabling Microsoft Defender, adding broad path/process exclusions, or stopping its services to enable follow-on tooling. Often performed via Set-MpPreference, registry edits, or BYOVD.

entries.name: DCShadow
entries.summary: Registering a rogue domain controller and pushing arbitrary AD object changes via replication. Bypasses normal change auditing and is used for stealth persistence (SID history, AdminSDHolder, krbtgt).

entries.name: Kerberos
entries.summary: Network authentication protocol used by Active Directory. Clients obtain a TGT from the KDC, then exchange it for service tickets (TGS) to access resources without resending credentials.

entries.name: NTLM
entries.summary: Microsoft challenge-response authentication protocol predating Kerberos. Still used for local accounts, workgroup auth, and fallback scenarios; vulnerable to relay and offline cracking.

entries.name: TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket)
entries.summary: Initial Kerberos ticket issued by the KDC after pre-authentication, encrypted with the krbtgt account's key. Presented to request service tickets (TGS) without re-authenticating.

entries.name: TGS (Service Ticket)
entries.summary: Kerberos service ticket issued by the KDC for a specific SPN, encrypted with the service account's key. Presented to the target service to authenticate the user.

entries.name: KDC (Key Distribution Center)
entries.summary: Kerberos service running on every domain controller. Comprises the Authentication Service (AS, issues TGTs) and the Ticket-Granting Service (TGS, issues service tickets).

entries.name: Krbtgt Account
entries.summary: Disabled domain account whose password hash encrypts every TGT issued in the domain. Compromise enables Golden Ticket forgery; remediation requires resetting the password twice.

entries.name: Active Directory
entries.summary: Microsoft's directory service for Windows domains. Stores users, computers, groups, GPOs, and security policy in a hierarchical structure (forest → domain → OU) replicated across domain controllers.

entries.name: Domain Controller
entries.summary: Windows server hosting Active Directory Domain Services. Authenticates users, enforces policy, replicates the AD database (NTDS.dit), and runs the KDC for Kerberos.

entries.name: LDAP
entries.summary: Protocol used to query and modify Active Directory (TCP 389, or 636 for LDAPS). Underpins AD enumeration tools (BloodHound, ADFind) and many privilege-escalation attacks against the directory.

entries.name: Group Policy (GPO)
entries.summary: Active Directory mechanism for centrally configuring user and computer settings. GPOs are stored in SYSVOL, linked to OUs/domains/sites, and applied at logon and refresh intervals.

entries.name: LAPS
entries.summary: Microsoft solution that randomizes the local Administrator password on each domain-joined host and stores it in a protected AD attribute. Reading the attribute is a high-value attacker target.

entries.name: Credential Guard
entries.summary: Windows feature that uses Virtualization-Based Security to isolate LSA secrets (NTLM hashes, Kerberos TGTs) in a hypervisor-protected process (lsaiso.exe). Blocks classic LSASS credential dumping.

entries.name: Sysmon
entries.summary: Sysinternals service and ETW provider that writes detailed process, network, file, registry, and image-load events to its own channel. Heavily used for endpoint visibility and detection engineering.

entries.name: C2 (Command and Control)
entries.summary: Channel an attacker uses to issue commands to and receive output from compromised hosts. Modern C2 commonly uses HTTPS, DNS, or domain-fronted transports with sleep/jitter to blend with normal traffic.

entries.name: Rclone
entries.summary: Open-source cloud storage sync utility frequently abused to exfiltrate data to attacker-controlled MEGA, Dropbox, S3, or WebDAV endpoints. Common in ransomware double-extortion playbooks.

entries.name: Phishing / Spear-phishing
entries.summary: Sending tailored email or message lures to trick users into opening attachments, clicking malicious links, or entering credentials on attacker-controlled pages. The dominant initial-access vector across intrusion data.

entries.name: RMM Abuse
entries.summary: Installing or co-opting legitimate remote monitoring and management software (AnyDesk, ScreenConnect, Atera, Splashtop, TeamViewer) for stealthy persistent access. Blends with admin activity and bypasses controls that block traditional C2 binaries.

entries.name: ACL / DACL Abuse
entries.summary: Exploiting permissive ACLs on AD objects (GenericAll, WriteDACL, WriteOwner, GenericWrite, WriteProperty) to take control of users, groups, computers, or GPOs. Core of BloodHound attack-path findings.

entries.name: RDP Session Hijacking
entries.summary: Using tscon (or service-based equivalents) from a SYSTEM context to attach to another user's disconnected RDP session without their password. Yields an authenticated interactive session as the target user.

entries.name: HTML Smuggling
entries.summary: Embedding an encoded payload inside an HTML page so it is reconstructed and saved by the user's browser via a JavaScript Blob. Bypasses email and web gateways that scan the originating HTML for malicious content.

entries.name: LNK / ISO Container Delivery
entries.summary: Wrapping a malicious LNK and supporting payload inside an ISO, IMG, or VHD container to evade Mark-of-the-Web propagation. Became a dominant delivery pattern after Microsoft began blocking macros from internet-sourced Office files.

entries.name: Process Hollowing
entries.summary: Spawning a legitimate process in a suspended state, unmapping its image, writing attacker code in its place, then resuming execution. The process appears to be the original benign binary in tooling that relies on the process name.

entries.name: PowerShell Logging Bypass
entries.summary: Disabling Script Block Logging, Module Logging, or transcription via reflection on PowerShell internals (e.g. setting GroupPolicySettings on the cached PSEtwLogProvider). Suppresses 4103/4104 records for follow-on commands in the same session.

entries.name: LSA Secrets
entries.summary: Encrypted secrets stored under HKLM\SECURITY\Policy\Secrets — including service account passwords, DPAPI machine keys, and cached domain credentials. Distinct from LSASS in-memory credentials and accessible with SYSTEM and SeBackupPrivilege.

entries.name: PowerShell Logs
entries.summary: PowerShell's three logging tiers — Module Logging (4103, pipeline parameters), Script Block Logging (4104, decoded script content), and Transcription (full session text). EID 800 on the Windows PowerShell channel records pipeline execution for legacy v2-style logs.

entries.name: WDAC / AppLocker
entries.summary: Microsoft application-control technologies that enforce policies on which executables, scripts, and DLLs may run. AppLocker is rule-based and user-mode; WDAC is policy-driven, kernel-enforced, and the recommended modern control.

entries.name: ASR (Attack Surface Reduction)
entries.summary: Microsoft Defender feature set that blocks common attack behaviors via per-rule policies — Office child processes, credential theft from LSASS, obfuscated scripts, USB execution, and more. Each rule has audit and block modes.

entries.name: LNK Files
entries.summary: Windows shell shortcut format pointing to a target path with arguments, working directory, icon, and machine identifiers. Used both as a phishing payload and as a forensic artifact recording user file access.

entries.name: ShellBags
entries.summary: Per-user registry artifacts (BagMRU, Bags) recording every folder a user has browsed in Explorer, including remote shares, removable media, and now-deleted folders. Survives folder deletion and proves user awareness of a path.

entries.name: Jump Lists
entries.summary: Per-application recently-used file lists stored under each user's profile (AutomaticDestinations and CustomDestinations). Records file paths, timestamps, and the AppID of the program that opened them.

entries.name: UserAssist
entries.summary: Per-user registry artifact (HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist) tracking GUI-launched programs with run counts and last-execution timestamps. Names are ROT13-encoded.

entries.name: Pagefile / Hiberfile
entries.summary: Disk files that hold paged-out (pagefile.sys) or hibernation (hiberfil.sys) memory contents. Frequently contain credentials, keys, and decoded payloads recoverable long after the originating process has exited.

entries.name: Mass File Encryption
entries.summary: Final ransomware impact phase: rapid enumeration and rewrite of user, server, and share files with an attacker-controlled extension, followed by a ransom note dropped into each affected directory. Often parallelized across local drives and SMB shares from a single staging host.
